title: Keys and Re‑rendering
description: How React uses keys to identify changes.

slides.title: Keys and Reconciliation 🔄
slides.content:
# How React Uses Keys

When a list updates, React compares the new list of keys with the previous list. 

* If a key exists in the new list but not in the old, React creates a new component.
* If a key exists in the old list but not in the new, React removes that component.
* If a key exists in both, React reuses the existing component (updating its props if needed).

This process is called **reconciliation**. Good keys prevent unnecessary re-renders and preserve component state (like input focus).

slides.title: Example: Without Keys
slides.content: Without keys, React has to guess. It often re-renders everything:

quizData.question: If keys are not provided, React will automatically generate unique keys.
quizData.explanation: False. React will warn you, and it will use the array index as a fallback, which can cause issues.
